cataloging_subject.label: Adam Davy's Five Dreams about Edward II and Other Middle English Verse (Furnivall's 1878 selection)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the edition-defined Middle English verse selection represented by aha2711, not one medieval work and not the complete physical manuscript or printed book. It contains five intellectual works: Adam Davy's Five Dreams; the Life of St Alexius represented by four versions in six manuscript streams; two non-contiguous excerpts from one Old Testament History; the Fifteen Signs before Doomsday with its manuscript-continuous Lamentacio animarum; and the separately identifiable Conception of Mary prologue beginning Off ioye & blisse. Furnivall's front matter, notes, index, and other back matter are outside the cataloged unit even where the XML preserves omission markers or editorial framing.

catalog_summary.title: Adam Davy's Five Dreams about Edward II and Other Middle English Verse (Furnivall's 1878 selection)
catalog_summary.author: Adam Davy (Five Dreams only); anonymous Middle English authors for the remaining works
catalog_summary.date: Five Dreams composed during Edward II's reign; edition-defined selection published 1878
catalog_summary.region: Component composition places unresolved; Furnivall's edition was published in London; the Laud 622 scribal language is localized to Essex at the witness layer only

titles.value: Adam Davy's Five Dreams about Edward II and Other Middle English Verse (Furnivall's 1878 selection)

assertion.notes: The qualifier makes the edition-defined mixed-work boundary explicit and is not part of the 1878 title.

titles.value: Adam Davy's 5 Dreams about Edward II. The Life of St. Alexius. Solomon's Book of Wisdom. St. Jeremie's 15 Tokens before Doomsday. The Lamentation of Souls

scope.description: Shortened transcription of the historical edition title

notes: The title does not name the separate Off ioye & blisse song or the sixty-four-line Solomon fragment even though both are represented.

titles.value: Adam Davy's Five Dreams about Edward II
Old Testament History, from Creation through Prophets, in the Temporale of the South English Legendary

assertion.notes: DIMEV's work-level identification prevents Furnivall's excerpt title King Solomon's Book of Wisdom from being mistaken for an autonomous Solomon-authored book.

titles.value: Conception of Mary (prologue: Off ioye & blisse is my song)

agents.name: Adam Davy
notes: The poem self-identifies Adam the marshal of Stratford-at-Bow; this responsibility is scoped to Five Dreams only.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English authors
notes: This collective display label does not imply common authorship of the four remaining works or their version branches.

assertion.notes: The attribution rests on repeated first-person self-naming within the unique Laud 622 witness and is accepted by the edition and DIMEV.

notes: Solomon and Saint Alexius are subjects, not authors; Furnivall reports but questions a traditional Jerome attribution for the Fifteen Signs, which is therefore not normalized as authorship.

assertion.notes: Furnivall states that the additional texts made the volume thick enough, selected six Alexius streams for parallel presentation, and moved the earlier Solomon passage after later manuscript material to complete his editorial Life of Solomon sequence.

date_statements.display: Compiled as Furnivall's EETS selection and published in 1878

assertion.notes: This date belongs to the edition-defined collection, not to composition of all medieval components.

date_statements.display: First published in this selection in 1878
During the reign of Edward II, 1307-1327

assertion.notes: The internal address to Sir Edward the king supports the regnal range; the end-fourteenth-century manuscript date is not substituted for composition.

scope.description: Rubrics and editorially retained Latin headings

form_statements.description: All five medieval intellectual works and all six Alexius streams are verse; editorial prose paragraphs and notes do not make the cataloged medieval selection mixed-form.

subject_statements.label: The Fifteen Signs before the Day of Judgment

summaries.value: Furnivall's 1878 EETS selection brings together five distinct Middle English verse works: Adam Davy's politically prophetic Five Dreams; four versions of the Life of St Alexius printed from six manuscripts; selected Old Testament History passages centered on Solomon and later biblical history; the Fifteen Signs and its continuous Lamentacio; and a separately copied prologue to the Conception of Mary. The selection is cataloged as an editorial collection while preserving each work, version branch, witness stream, and non-contiguous excerpt as a scoped part.

The XML represents the medieval texts on printed pp. 11-98 and retains short editorial frames, but marks the half title, most front matter, and pp. 99-122 back matter as omitted. Its 124 unique page-image references are carrier links, while the 424 PB occurrences arise chiefly because the six Alexius streams are encoded in parallel. Six verse lines quoted inside editorial NOTE1 elements are excluded from the intellectual-work metrics.

extent.description: Five intellectual works are counted once at the root level. Their represented XML totals are 529 LG and 4,735 intrinsic L elements: Five Dreams 4/166; six Alexius witness streams 513/4,069; the two Old Testament History excerpts 6/360; Fifteen Signs plus Lamentacio 5/54; and the Conception prologue 1/86. The prologue's 86 XML L elements each contain two printed short lines, yielding 172 printed short-line positions; DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 describes the broader work as beginning with an 84-line prologue, so those source-specific conventions are not equated. The XML-wide 4,741 L total additionally includes six verse lines quoted inside editorial NOTE1 elements; those are excluded. Child dreams, version branches, streams, and excerpt portions have item_count 0 so they do not inflate the five-work count. The 17 P and 424 PB elements are editorial/carrier structures, not works; 124 PB REF values are unique.

content_parts.label: Adam Davy's Five Dreams about Edward II
content_parts.summary: One 166-line poem containing five dream episodes, not five independently counted works; DIMEV record 5993 identifies the unique Laud 622 witness.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem'][1]; 166 intrinsic L in 4 LG; printed pp. 11-16

content_parts.label: First dream: Edward attacked before St Edward's shrine
content_parts.summary: Target L positions 1-42; the attack is followed by colored bands springing from the king's ears.

locators.value: Five Dreams target L 1-42, excluding nested NOTE1 quotations

content_parts.label: Second dream: Edward rides barefoot toward Rome
content_parts.summary: Target L positions 43-66, introduced as Anoþer sweuene.

content_parts.label: Third dream: imperial coronation at Rome
content_parts.summary: Target L positions 67-86, explicitly introduced as þe þrid sweuene.

content_parts.label: Fourth dream: Christ accompanies his knight
content_parts.summary: Target L positions 87-134; this dream shares an XML LG container with the third dream, so the textually signaled boundary is preserved without inventing an XML division.

content_parts.label: Fifth dream: the angel and the bleeding lamb
content_parts.summary: Target L positions 135-166, followed by Adam Davy's command to report the vision.

content_parts.label: Life of St Alexius: four versions from six manuscripts
content_parts.summary: One abstract hagiographic work represented by four Middle English version branches and six complete witness streams; the parent is counted once and all version/stream children have item_count 0.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='legend']; printed pp. 17-80; 4,069 L in 513 LG across six streams
four versions from six manuscripts, printed in parallel on pp. 20-79

content_parts.label: Version 1: twelve-line-stanza Laud 622 version
content_parts.summary: DIMEV record 382; one witness stream in Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 622.

content_parts.label: Version 2: couplet Cotton Titus A XXVI version
content_parts.summary: DIMEV record 379; one witness stream in British Library Cotton MS Titus A XXVI.

content_parts.label: Version 3: earlier six-line-stanza version
content_parts.summary: DIMEV record 4923; Furnivall prints the Vernon and Laud 108 streams in parallel.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='legend']/DIV2[3]; 1,224 L in 204 LG across two streams

content_parts.label: Vernon Manuscript stream
content_parts.summary: The complete 612-line Vernon copy of the earlier six-line-stanza version.

content_parts.label: Laud Misc. 108 stream
content_parts.summary: The complete 612-line Laud 108 copy of the earlier six-line-stanza version.

content_parts.label: Version 4: later six-line-stanza version
content_parts.summary: DIMEV record 3102; Furnivall prints Laud 463 and Trinity College Oxford MS 57 in parallel.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='legend']/DIV2[4]; 1,271 L in 212 LG across two streams

content_parts.label: Laud Misc. 463 stream
content_parts.summary: The 635-line Laud 463 copy of the later six-line-stanza version.

content_parts.label: Trinity College Oxford MS 57 stream
content_parts.summary: A 636-line stream beginning at f. 73; Furnivall's milestones run through f. 77v, but the exact current-catalog endpoint remains unverified.

locators.value: begins f. 73; XML milestones through f. 77v; endpoint unverified

content_parts.label: Old Testament History selection
content_parts.summary: One work, DIMEV record 6345, represented by two Laud 622 excerpts that Furnivall separates and reorders: a 296-line continuation beginning with Solomon's counsel and a preceding 64-line Solomon passage appended later in the edition.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem'][2] and BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='fragment']; printed pp. 81-90 and 96-98
f. 69 col. 1 and ff. 69v-70v within the larger imperfect Old Testament History at ff. 65r-70v

content_parts.label: King Solomon's Book of Wisdom
content_parts.summary: Furnivall's excerpt title for a 296-line portion that continues beyond Solomon through Rehoboam, Elijah, Elisha, Daniel, Doom, and Antichrist; it is not an autonomous biblical book authored by Solomon.

content_parts.label: Solomon's Coronation, Deeds, Judgment, Court, and Temple
content_parts.summary: A 64-line passage that physically precedes the Book of Wisdom excerpt in Laud 622 but is moved to pp. 96-98 by Furnivall; a still earlier birth passage is deliberately omitted.

content_parts.label: Fifteen Signs before Doomsday with Lamentacio animarum
content_parts.summary: One manuscript-continuous eschatological unit of 54 long-line XML elements: the first 22 describe the Fifteen Signs and the following 32 continue through the Lamentacio rubric without a narrative break.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem'][3]/DIV2[1] and first 32 L of DIV2[2]; printed pp. 91-93

content_parts.label: Fifteen Signs before Doomsday
content_parts.summary: The opening 22 long lines, traditionally attributed to Jerome in the rubric and edition discussion but not cataloged as Jerome's authorship.

content_parts.label: Lamentacio animarum continuation
content_parts.summary: Thirty-two long lines following the rubric; Furnivall explicitly notes that the manuscript has no break in the story.

content_parts.label: Off ioye & blisse is my song: Conception of Mary prologue
content_parts.summary: A separately copied prologue-only witness identified by DIMEV record 4178. The XML has 86 long-line elements, each representing two printed short lines, or 172 printed short-line positions; DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 instead describes the work's added prologue generically as 84 lines.

evidence.citation: XPath and structural audit of pinned aha2711 XML, 2026-07-16

evidence.summary: Supports the edition boundary, editor/compilation responsibility, contents, manuscript sources, exact printed loci, Alexius version model, Solomon reordering, and Fifteen Signs/Lamentacio distinction.

evidence.summary: Supports the digital item identity, bibliographic title, publication, subject terms, contents, and public-domain statement.

open_questions.question: Did Furnivall transcribe every collateral Alexius stream directly from the physical manuscripts, or did any stream pass through Horstmann's transcripts or earlier print?
next_steps: Inspect Furnivall's surviving correspondence, transcripts, and EETS production papers.
Compare distinctive readings and errors against Horstmann's earlier Laud 108 printing before adding any intermediary derivation edge.

open_questions.question: What is the exact current-foliation endpoint of the Trinity College Oxford MS 57 Alexius stream?
next_steps: Inspect the Gameson catalog record and physical or supplied images around ff. 73-78.
Reconcile Furnivall's milestones through f. 77v with DIMEV's start-only locator.

open_questions.question: Why does DIMEV report 318 edition lines for the Old Testament History selection while the two encoded excerpts contain 296 plus 64, or 360, intrinsic L elements?
next_steps: Audit DIMEV's edition-locus/count convention against the 1878 printed lineation and its two non-contiguous excerpts.

open_questions.question: Should DIMEV record 4622's 22-line description be read as only the Fifteen Signs opening even though its Laud 622 first/last lines and edition locus encompass the 54-line manuscript-continuous Signs plus Lamentacio?
next_steps: Compare DIMEV's record-construction rules and all witnesses of record 4622.
Preserve the XML and Furnivall boundary evidence rather than silently forcing either count.

open_questions.question: How should DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1's generic 84-line Conception prologue description be reconciled with the Laud 622 encoding's 86 long-line elements and 172 printed short-line positions?
next_steps: Compare the complete witnesses and DIMEV's line-count convention with Furnivall's printed numbering.
Keep the aha2711 encoding count and the DIMEV work-description count source-scoped meanwhile.

open_questions.question: Can Bodleian supply an existing complete digital surrogate of MS. Laud Misc. 622 beyond the single public 35mm slide?
next_steps: Ask the Bodleian mediated copying service about an internal surrogate or new photography of the target folios.
Request ff. 21v-27v and 69r-71v first, while distinguishing those target leaves from the whole codex.

notes: Furnivall rejects the older attribution of all Laud 622 poems to Adam Davy; only Five Dreams is assigned to Davy here.
Saint Jerome's name is retained as a historical rubric/title feature, not normalized authorship; Furnivall himself notes that the Fifteen Signs text was not found in the Benedictine Jerome edition.
The Laud 622 end-fourteenth-century witness date and Essex dialect localization are witness-level facts, not shared composition metadata for all five works.
The complete Vernon and Laud 108 image bundles verify the exact Alexius loci, but image rights remain separate from the public-domain medieval text and edition layers.
All metrics are reproducible encoding counts and semantic boundary decisions, not a claim that XML L always equals one short verse line or that editorial parallelism creates additional works.
